glossary.def: A quantile of the loss distribution: the loss a portfolio will not exceed with probability α over a chosen horizon. The 99% one-day VaR is the threshold worse than only 1 day in 100 — a single number that says nothing about how bad the breaches themselves are.
The average loss conditional on being in the worst (1−α) tail — the mean of all losses beyond VaR. It is always at least as large as VaR at the same level and, unlike VaR, is a coherent risk measure.

A risk measure satisfying Artzner et al.'s four axioms: monotonicity, translation invariance, positive homogeneity, and subadditivity. Expected Shortfall is coherent; VaR is not, because it can violate subadditivity.

The axiom ρ(A+B) ≤ ρ(A)+ρ(B): combining portfolios cannot increase total risk, so diversification is never penalized. VaR can break this — reporting a merged book as riskier than the sum of its parts — which is the central case against it.

Estimating VaR/ES directly from the empirical sample of past returns by sorting and reading off the order statistic, with no distributional assumption. It captures real fat tails and skew but is blind to any risk the lookback window never contained.

A likelihood-ratio test of a VaR model's unconditional coverage: it compares the model's claimed exception rate p against the observed rate x/T, with the statistic distributed χ²(1). Reject the model when LR exceeds 3.841 (the 95% critical value).

The supervisory backtest bucketing a bank by 99% VaR exceptions over 250 days: green (0–4, accepted), yellow (5–9, capital multiplier raised), red (10+, model presumed flawed). The boundaries follow the Binomial(250, 0.01) distribution.

Repricing a portfolio under specific historical or hypothetical scenarios — or, in reverse stress testing, solving for the scenario that breaks the firm — without attaching a probability. It is the deliberate complement to statistical VaR/ES, covering events the distribution never sampled.

Christoffersen, P. F. (1998). Evaluating Interval Forecasts. International Economic Review 39(4).
Rockafellar, R. T. & Uryasev, S. (2000). Optimization of Conditional Value-at-Risk. Journal of Risk 2(3).
Hull, J. C. (2021). Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives (11th ed.). Pearson.
